Good morning and thank you for the introduction. It is a pleasure to be back at the Brookings Institution today. I want to share some observations about the economy and the path of monetary policy. Let me begin with the labor market and then turn to inflation. The committee has emphasized that we will be patient as we assess incoming data. We continue to be data dependent in our approach to setting policy. Recent indicators suggest the economy is moderating in line with our expectations. Job gains have remained solid but are gradually slowing. Wage growth is moderating toward levels consistent with our two percent objective. We have made good progress on the inflation side over the past year. Goods prices have been softening for several quarters now. Services inflation, while still elevated, is also showing signs of moderation. Inflation expectations remain well anchored across most measures we monitor. The committee believes risks to the dual mandate have moved into better balance. We are well-positioned to respond as the outlook evolves. Our policy stance is appropriately calibrated for the current environment. Households continue to spend carefully but at a healthy pace. Business investment has been moderating in interest sensitive sectors. Financial conditions have eased somewhat over recent months. Credit growth has been moderating in a measured fashion. Housing activity is showing tentative signs of stabilization. Manufacturing output is approaching balance with services demand. The unemployment rate has drifted up only gradually. Labor force participation has improved in a balanced way. Productivity growth has been encouraging in recent quarters. Real incomes are gradually recovering as inflation moderates. Consumer sentiment surveys show households remain cautiously optimistic. The committee will proceed carefully with any further policy adjustments. We remain data dependent and will move gradually as conditions warrant. The progress on inflation has been considerable progress over the cycle. We see encouraging signs across multiple components of the price basket. Survey based inflation expectations remain well anchored. Market based measures of inflation compensation are similarly contained. Global disinflation has been a tailwind for our domestic outlook. Supply chains have largely normalized after years of disruption. Energy markets have been moderating after earlier spikes. Food prices have been softening at the wholesale level. Used vehicle prices have been moderating sharply. Owners equivalent rent is finally moderating in the data. Healthcare services inflation is gradually approaching balance. Education costs are moderating relative to recent trends. We are encouraged but not yet fully satisfied with the progress. The committee is committed to returning inflation sustainably to two percent. We will be patient and carefully assess each meetings worth of data. Risks remain in both directions and we are balanced in our approach. The labor market is gradually approaching balance with our objectives. Wage pressures are moderating in line with productivity gains. Demand and supply are approaching balance across many sectors. Financial stability indicators remain reassuring on the whole. Bank lending standards have stabilized after earlier tightening. Now let me turn to the risks ahead and what they may imply for policy. Recent data on services prices has surprised to the upside. The latest employment cost index came in firmer than expected. Core inflation in the most recent month accelerated meaningfully. The committee judges that further firming may be warranted. Further firming of policy is appropriate given the persistent pressures. We must remain restrictive for a sustained period to ensure progress. Additional firming will likely be needed to bring inflation down. The committee stands ready to act decisively if pressures build. We are observing elevated price pressures in core services categories. Inflation persistent in housing and medical services components remains a concern. The tight labor market continues to feed wage and price dynamics. Persistent inflation in services categories has not yet moderated sufficiently. We will be vigilant and act decisively to preserve price stability. Policy firming additional firming is on the table at upcoming meetings. The committee judges that more restrictive policy is warranted. We have more work to do to fully restore price stability. There is a long way to go before we can declare victory. Inflation remains too high relative to our two percent objective. The robust labor market gives us scope to act decisively. Further firming additional firming will be considered carefully but resolutely. Remain restrictive remain restrictive is our watchword for now. We will act decisively if elevated price pressures persist. Policy firming is warranted given the persistent inflation backdrop. The tight labor market and elevated price pressures argue for more restrictive policy. Further tightening cannot be ruled out at the next meeting. The committee will not hesitate to undertake additional firming as needed. We are prepared to act decisively to defend the inflation target. More restrictive policy and further firming are clearly on the table. Persistent inflation in core services warrants additional firming soon. The path ahead requires us to remain restrictive and vigilant. Thank you for your attention and I look forward to your questions.
